description:
This course covers the essentials of Knee MRI. It includes an in-depth introductory video covering anatomy & what to look for. It also includes over 30 essential cases that cover what you need to know -- including links to key findings, learning material, and pearls. Happy Learning!
Course Expert MSK Radiologist Consultant: Dr. Angela Atinga. 
Thank you to Dr. Rakesh Mohankumar, expert MSK radiologist, for help with course creation.

data.text: IT Band inserting at Gerdy\u0027s Tubercle

teachingSections.name: Menisci
teachingSections.html:
C-shaped fibrocartilaginous structures between femur and tibia, the 'shock absorbers'.

Appearance: They are uniformly low signal on all sequences, but may see horizontal gray signal (small vessel) that is normal (not a tear).

Anatomy:

  • Medial and lateral meniscus on axial images. Notice the lateral meniscus has a more 'open-faced C shape' (ends are wider apart)
  • Each has a posterior horn, body, anterior horn, and root attachments.
  • The posterior horn of medial meniscus is larger than anterior horn; this is more symmetrical for lateral
  • Practice identifying the root attachments (anterior root lateral meniscus, posterior root lateral meniscus, anterior root medial meniscus, posterior root lateral meniscus), as root tears are commonly missed

teachingSections.name: Cruciate Ligaments
teachingSections.html:
Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL)

Two bundles (anteromedial and posterolateral) are named based on their insertion on the tibia.

  • Anteromedial bundle attaches to roof of intercondylar notch superiorly (inserts anteromedial)
  • Posterolateral bundle attaches more laterally to wall of intercondylar notch (inserts posterolateral)
  • Inserts on tibial plateau / tibial spine
  • Normal striated appearance
  • Notice the anterior root of lateral meniscus inserts in close proximity to the ACL (inseparable)


Posterior Cruciate Ligament (PCL)

PCL is more uniformly low signal.

  • Arises from anterolateral surface of medial femoral condyle
  • Extends posterolaterally
  • Inserts at posterior tibial plateau
  • Notice that the anterior aspect of PCL is bordered by the posterior horn of medial meniscus
  • Notice meniscofemoral ligaments (Humphrey and Wrisberg) anterior and posterior to PCL, a normal variant. The clefts between this and the PCL can mimic a tear (but this is a normal structure)

teachingSections.name: Collateral Ligaments, Posteromedial and Posterolateral Corner
teachingSections.html:
Medial Collateral Ligament (MCL)

Composed of superficial and deep components:

  • Superficial MCL: thick, superficial, long component arising from posterosuperior medial femoral epicondyle and inserting distally at two sites medial tibia (often not entirely seen on Coronal images). The Superficial MCL is also often called the "Tibial Collateral Ligament"
  • Deep MCL: meniscotibial and meniscofemoral components, seen as a thickening of the joint capsule

Posteromedial Corner:

  • Semimembranosus - broad, fanlike tendon inserting at posteromedial tibia. Follow from proximal to distal.
  • Medial gastrocnemius tendon. Note that a baker's cyst typically sits between these two structures
  • Pes Anserine Tendons - "Say Grace before Tea" (Sartorius, Gracilis, Semitendinosus)


Lateral Collateral Ligament Complex, Posterolateral Corner:

  • Lateral Collateral Ligament (LCL), aka Fibular Collateral Ligament - follow from femoral (posterosuperior lateral femoral epicondyle) to insertion on anterolateral fibular head. Follow on axial images: proximal, mid, distal
  • Biceps Femoris Tendon - follow it down to its split attachment onto fibula, with LCL passing through this

Together, Biceps Femoris Tendon and LCL are referred to as the Conjoint Tendon (see Sag images)

  • Popliteus Tendon can be followed from the popliteus muscle superiorly, where it wraps around the lateral knee to insert on lateral femoral condyle. We can also see this well on coronal images towards its insertion where there is often magic angle artifact

Other structures to note:

  • IT Band inserting at Gerdy's Tubercle (also see more proximal)
  • Lateral Gastrocnemius Tendon

teachingSections.name: Extensor Mechanism
teachingSections.html:
• Quadriceps Tendon - trilaminar appearance due to three slips from superficial to deep: rectus femoris, vastus medialis/lateralis, vastus intermedius.
  • Relatively broad on axial images
  • Patellar Tendon - from inferior patella to tibial tuberosity. Also see on axial images

Other related structures to be aware of:

  • MPFL (Medial Patellofemoral Ligament) and Medial Retinaculum, often torn in lateral patellar dislocation

teachingSections.name: Articular Cartilage
teachingSections.html:
• Notice normal intermediate signal
  • Assess all three compartments (patellofemoral - including lateral and medial patellar cartilage on axial images, Femoral Trochlear cartilage). Also seen well on Sagittal images
  • Tibiofemoral cartilage also well seen on Sag, partially on coronal (4 unlabeled arrows)

teachingSections.name: Joint Fluid & Synovium
teachingSections.html:
There are more specifically named recesses, but for now, consider the following general anatomy:

  • Suprapatellar Recess (between fat pads in midline), extends medial and lateral (also see axial)
  • Posterior Recesses (named recesses include PCL recess, posterior femoral recesses)
  • Medial and Lateral Recesses
  • Central Synovial Recess
  • See joint fluid and synovium

teachingSections.name: Popliteal Fossa, Neurovascular
teachingSections.html:
• Typical location of Baker's Cyst
  • Neurovascular Structures: Popliteal Artery, Vein, and Sciatic Nerve
  • Follow Sciatic Nerve down, bifurcates into Tibial and Common Fibular Nerves (follow more distally)
  • Short Saphenous Vein drains into popliteal vein
  • Lymph Nodes

teachingSections.name: Muscles
teachingSections.html:
• Largely covered in previous sections, but labelled here.
  • Gastrocnemius (introduced earlier), Popliteus, and Anterolateral Compartment
  • Vastus Medialis, Vastus Lateralis (notice relatively larger medialis at the level of the knee, another marker of the medial aspect of the knee)
  • Biceps Femoris, Semimembranosus, and Sartorius/Gracilis/Semitendinosus muscles also visible

teachingSections.name: Fat Pads
teachingSections.html:
• Anterior Suprapatellar (Quadriceps)
  • Posterior Suprapatellar (Prefemoral)
  • Infrapatellar (Hoffa's)

Notice suprapatellar recess sits between the quadriceps and prefemoral fat pad, giving the classic appearance of an effusion on knee Xray.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Tear in the posterior horn of the medial meniscus (fluid signal reaching articular surface on at least 2 slices)
  • Predominantly horizontal morphology (parallel to tibial plateau and separating meniscus into superior and inferior components). Also see coronal images (Cor PD, and Cor PD FS)
  • Parameniscal cysts (also see their relationship to the tear on coronal by scrolling)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
As the first meniscal tear we are showing, we will cover some basic concepts here that will apply to many other cases. But, this case demonstrates the appearance of a horizontal meniscal tear.

Basic Concept: Is there a Tear?

  • To call a tear, you want to see bright signal in the meniscus that reaches the articular surface on at least 2 slices. This case demonstrates these findings, confirming a tear.
  • "Reaching the articular surface" means it extends to touch the articular cartilage surface

Basic Concept: What type of Tear?

We classify meniscal tears based on morphology. The morphology has implications, including for treatment planning. There isn't a standard classification system, but they include:

  • Longitudinal
  • Vertical - perpendicular to tibial plateau (splits meniscus into medial and lateral)
  • Horizontal - parallel to tibial plateau (like this case, splitting into superior and inferior)
  • Radial (from central to peripheral)
  • Root (a tear involving the root of meniscus, often a radial tear)
  • Complex (combination of types)
  • Other (Parrot-beak, Displaced Fragment, Bucket-handle)

We discuss horizontal tears (this case) in the next section.

teachingSections.name: Horizontal Tears
teachingSections.html:
Horizontal tears (the predominant pattern in this case) are the most common pattern of tear, running parallel to the tibial plateau and separating meniscus into superior and inferior parts. They reach the articular surface or the free edge.

Things to know:

  • Occur in older patients (>40), common in degenerative disease
  • Can reach the periphery of meniscus, and therefore can have parameniscal cysts (like this case)
  • Treatment, when required, can involve repair, debridement, partial meniscectomy, and/or decompression of parameniscal cysts.

Other topics touched on in this case:

  • Parameniscal cysts are small cysts of syovial fluid adjacent to a meniscal tear that extend beyond the margin of the meniscus. If you see these, look closely for a meniscal tear adjacent. The reverse is true (look for the cysts when you have a tear that reaches the periphery such as horizontal or radial tear).
  • They can be symptomatic, and may require decompression.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 3 - Longitudinal Vertical tear (& complete ACL tear)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Menisci:

  • Small longitudinal vertical tear of the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus (and here, here).
  • Linear region of high signal in the posterior aspect of the medial meniscus which extends to the articular surface, in keeping with longitudinal oblique (mostly horizontal) undersurface tear. Adjacent parameniscal cyst
  • Notice clefts between normal structures that are NOT tears (i.e. cleft between transverse ligament and meniscus)

Other Findings covered in other cases:

  • Full thickness tear of the ACL (complete discontinuity)
  • Bone marrow edema in the lateral condylar notch, likely from prior pivot shift injury
  • Multifocal chondral fissuring of the medial patellar facet

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case demonstrates a small vertical tear laterally and an oblique/horizontal tear medially. We show this case to demonstrate the appearance of a vertical tear, and differentiate this from normal clefts. We will discuss the other findings (ACL tear, chondral defects, pivot-shift mechanism) in other cases.


﻿Vertical longitudinal tears are perpendicular to the tibial plateau, and run along longitudinal fibres -- thus separating the meniscus into medial and lateral parts. They often occur in younger patients post trauma, often peripheral and in posterior horns. What else to know:

  • Appearance is vertical high signal that reaches one or both articular surfaces
  • Strong association between peripheral longitudinal tears and ACL Tears, like in this case (see below).
  • Posterior lateral meniscal tears, like the one we showed, can be challenging to identify given the complex anatomy here.

These are more likely to be repaired, and often involve the red zone of meniscus. Recall the white zone (centrally) is poorly vascularized, whereas the red zone (peripheral) is well vascularized and more amenable to repair.


ACL tears and Posterior Horn Meniscal Tears:

In an ACL tear, there is anterior tibial translation that can result in traction injury between the menisci and the joint capsule / ligaments. For the lateral meniscus, this often results in a vertical tear between the part of the posterior horn attached to meniscofemoral ligaments and the remainder. A similar phenomenon can happen in the medial meniscus (ramp lesion).

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 4 - Radial Tear (also a Root Tear)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Large full thickness radial tear at posterior root attachment of the medial meniscus (also see Cor PD)
  • On Sag, there is loss of normal meniscal signal at the posterior root of the medial meniscus, with a faint marching cleft (see this cleft here, here, here)
  • Associated meniscal extrusion (extending beyond the joint, compare with lateral)

Other Findings (Covered elsewhere):

  • Small ganglion cyst at femoral attachment of ACL, suggesting prior low to intermediate grade injury (also see Sag)
  • Moderate chondrosis of the medial facet and apex of the patella with focal area of full thickness fissure (see how thin the medial cartilage is compared to lateral as well). Mild chondrosis of the trochlear sulcus.
  • Tiny popliteal (Baker's) cyst

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Radial Meniscal Tears are those that extend radially from free edge toward the periphery. With complete radial tears, there is transection of longitudinal fibres and loss of hoop strength with meniscal extrusion (meniscus extends beyond the joint).

The most common locations are:

  • Posterior horn of medial meniscus (like this case)
  • Junction of anterior horn and body of lateral meniscus
  • Posterior horn of lateral meniscus near the root in the context of ACL tear (lateral meniscus oblique radial tear; LMORT)


Imaging Signs in Radial Tears:

The imaging signs all reflect the nature of the tear, from central to peripheral along the short axis. Which sign you see will depend on how we 'catch it' on the plane of imaging. We introduce them with schematics below.

  1. "Truncated Triangle Sign" - abrupt termination of the triangle at the tip
  2. "Cleft Sign" - vertical high signal that extends through the meniscus on Cor or Sag images
  3. "Marching Cleft Sign" - tear that 'marches' from central to peripheral on Sag or Cor images
  4. "Ghost Meniscus Sign" - absence of normal dark meniscus signal, with high signal replacing normal meniscal signal.

These might be tough to wrap your head around at first, but the schematics in this image are helpful to conceptualize why these signs occur in radial tears (A is truncated triangle, B is cleft sign, C is marching cleft, and D is ghost meniscus sign). The AJR article (Harper et al. AJR. https://ajronline.org/doi/10.2214/AJR.04.1024) is also helpful, and includes MR examples of these appearances.


This is also a "Root Tear", which is (often) a radial tear involving the root attachment, like in this case.

Normally, you should see a normal medial meniscal root one slice medial to the PCL (here it is abnormal). This is a common location to spot a root tear.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Meniscal tear involving posterior horn of medial meniscus with radial component (see truncated triangle, cleft sign as scrolling through). See axial images too
  • Oblique component (follow that to here) with flipped meniscal fragment superior to the posterior insertion adjacent to the PCL

Other Findings:

  • Small volume joint effusion.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A "Parrot Beak Tear" is a tear that classically starts radial and then continues into a longitudinal manner towards the periphery (hence the curved shape of a parrot beak). This produces an unstable meniscal fragment that can flip, like in this case.

Displaced tears, like this one, are important to identify before procedures. They can lead to locking of the knee. You should know:

  • They involve medial meniscus much more frequently (like this case)
  • Most medial meniscal displaced fragments end up posteriorly near the PCL, like in this case; but can end up in intercondylar notch or superior recess

These often require debridement or re-attachment.

cases.clinicalHistory: Prior ACL repair, medial meniscectomy. R/O other abnormality.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Tear involving the posterior root attachment of the lateral meniscus (also see here and scroll), with 'ghost meniscus' at the root. There is also some apical blunting and fraying of the mid body and posterior horn.

Other findings:

  • Previous ACL reconstruction with a bone tendon bone graft. There is partial attenuation of the anterior cruciate ligament graft close to the tibial attachment with vertical orientation proximally and more horizontal orientation distally - compatible with notch impingement. Follow ACL graft here to here.
  • Note we will discuss post-ACL findings in a later case.
  • Diminutive medial meniscus, with known prior partial menisectomy (there was a prior MRI, and findings were unchanged)
  • Mild to moderate chondral thinning involving the medial tibiofemoral joint compartment. The lateral tibiofemoral articular cartilage demonstrates moderate chondrosis (notice abnormal signal and contour compared to medial). Multifocal areas of mild to moderate patellofemoral chondrosis is also identified.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case demonstrates another root tear, this time involving the lateral meniscus. There are many other findings here that are described above, including related to the ACL repair, that we will discuss in more detail in a future case.


Recall that Root Tears are often radial tears that involve the meniscal root. Coronal fluid sensitive sequences help identify these abnormalities.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Large bucket-handle tear of the medial meniscus with large displaced fragment parallel to PCL (double PCL)
  • Double anterior horn, with part of meniscal fragment seen posterior to the anterior horn
  • The posterior horn of medial meniscus (usually larger than anterior) is diminutive
  • Although continuous bowtie is seen of the body, it is abnormal / diminutive
  • Scroll through the Coronal and Sag images to identify the flipped fragment. Compare with schematics in the learning material to understand the appearance here.

Other Findings:

  • Extensor tendons intact. Mild thickening of the proximal patellar tendon with focal increased T2-signal intensity along the central dorsal aspect of the proximal patellar tendon in keeping with low grade acute upon chronic patellar tendinosis.
  • Low grade partial tearing of the ACL (wavy, intrasubstance cyst inferiorly)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A Bucket-Handle Tear is a longitudinal/ vertical tear with migration of the inner/medial component. It is much more common with the medial meniscus.

Described Signs in Buckethandle Tears:

  • Absent Bow Tie Sign - meniscal body is flipped, so do not see an intact full bow tie for 2 slices on Sag
  • Fragment in intercondylar notch - displacement of the fragment to middle of joint but eccentric to PCL
  • Double PCL - displacement of fragment parallel to PCL (like our case)
  • Double anterior horn or flipped meniscus - meniscal fragment seen posterior to anterior horn (like in our case)
  • Disproportionately small posterior horn (like in our case)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Lateral Meniscus is Discoid, without a tear
  • Notice that the meniscal body is seen on several Sag slices (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and so on) -- giving a 'complete bow tie' appearance on several slices
  • The size of the meniscal body is well demonstrated on Coronal images

Other Findings:

  • Previous IM nailing of the tibia. The superior aspect of the nail is within the field of view and is creating metal susceptibility artifact, degrading the image quality. Notice the STIR images are comparatively less impacted by metal artifact, but have lower spatial resolution.
  • There is a surgical scar along the anteromedial aspect of the knee (Scarring of the anteromedial aspect of the knee including the inferior patellar fat pad related to a previous tibial nailing)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Discoid Meniscus refers to enlarged meniscus that extends centrally, MUCH more common in lateral meniscus (like our case). The diagnosis can be made on imaging when meniscal body > 15 mm, or can see full bow tie on 3 or more Sag slices (with standard 4 mm slices).

Discoid meniscus can be classified as:

  • Complete - block shaped and covers the entire tibial plateau
  • Partial - covers 80% or less of tibial plateau
  • Wrisberg Variant (Unstable) - thickened posterior horn lacking posterior attachments (meniscocapsular ligaments); can cause snapping knee syndrome

cases.clinicalHistory: Injury during soccer, heard a pop

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Complete tear at the proximal third of the ACL. There is complete loss of contiguity, also seen on axial images (follow from superior attachment and inferiorly with frank discontiguity with more inferior aspect
  • Bone marrow edema noted along the posterior lateral tibial plateau and in the mid lateral femoral condyle (with slight deepening of the lateral sulcus terminalis), in keeping with kissing contusions.
  • There is also a small area of bone marrow edema noted along the medial most aspect of the medial femoral condyle likely related to bony contusion.
  • Moderate to large knee joint effusion
  • Disrupted popliteomeniscal fascicle (a small ligament that stabilizes lateral meniscus by anchoring it to popliteus tendon)

Overall, findings are in keeping with complete ACL tear. Kissing contusions along the lateral femoral condyle lateral tibial plateau. No meniscus injury. Moderate to large joint effusion.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
The ACL is the most commonly reconstructed knee ligament. MRI is highly sensitive and specific for ACL Tear. The classic history is a non-contact injury with plant and twist, a popping sensation, immediate swelling with inability to weight bear; instability on exam.


Direct Signs:

  • The most common appearance of complete tear is frank focal discontinuity of ACL fibres (like in our case)
  • Abnormal ACL orientation with more horizontal orientation of distal component and vertical orientation of proximal component.

Partial ACL Tears more commonly involve the anteromedial bundle. Accuracy of MRI is lower for partial injuries and more variable -- with high specificity but modest sensitivity.

In pediatric patients, you are more likely to see an avulsion fracture than an intrasubstance ligament tear -- but this is a functional ACL tear.


Secondary Signs:

  • Bone contusions from pivot-shift injury -- as seen in our case -- with involvement of lateral femoral condyle and posterolateral tibial plateau
  • Deepening of lateral sulcus (also seen on xray) > 1.5 mm
  • Anterior tibial translation (> 7 mm)
  • Bowing of PCL

Other associations:

  • Segond fracture
  • "Terrible Triad" with MCL injury and medial meniscal tear
  • Meniscocapsular injury (Wrisberg rip, ramp lesion, meniscocapsular separation)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• There is significant expansion and increased signal intensity of the ACL in keeping with mucoid degeneration.
  • See intact fibres on T2W images, but can also follow on PD sequences (i.e. Cor). This gives a "celery stalk" appearance due to longitudinal fibres

Other findings (not primary reason for showing this case):

  • Tear of the body and anterior horn of the lateral meniscus. The anterior horn is macerated. No flipped meniscal fragments.
  • There is intrasubstance degeneration of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus but no discrete tear clefts identified.
  • Mild tendinopathy at the insertion of biceps femoris.
  • There is a moderate joint effusion with synovitis (notice the frond-like synovial hypertrophy indicating synovitis)
  • Chondral changes (tricompartmental)

Overall, there is a lateral meniscal tear, ACL mucoid degeneration (reason for showing this case), tendinopathy at the insertion of the biceps femoris, and tricompartmental degenerative changes.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Mucoid Degeneration of the ACL is a relatively common cause of increased signal in the ACL. You need to know about this as it can clearly mimic a tear. The typical appearance is:

  • Thickened and ill-defined ACL with a "celery stalk" appearance
  • Intact fibres visible, best seen on T2W
  • Absence of secondary findings of ACL injury -- bony contusions, meniscal tears, etc

This is rare in younger patients, so consider a tear in a young active patient when equivocal.

cases.clinicalHistory: Injury post fall. Clinically complete MCL and positive Lachmann's
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 11 - PCL Tear (& MCL complete disruption)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Rupture of PCL (complete tear), with frank discontinuity, and also thickening and increased signal.
  • Complete tear of the the superficial and meniscofemoral deep MCL from the femoral attachment, retracted by approximately 1 cm, with small hematoma between the torn fibres and the femur. No avulsion fracture. The meniscotibial component appears preserved.
  • Follow the MCL on axial images from inferior to site of complete discontiguity / complete tear
  • The mid fibres of MPFL towards femoral attachment are also partially torn. Fluid tracks between the torn MCL and the femur, including extending posteriorly along the superficial fascia of the vastus medialis.
  • The proximal, anterior fibres of the ACL and abnormally hyperintense and likely partially torn, with no fibre laxity. No complete tear. No significant tibial translation.
  • Marrow edema of the posterior, lateral femoral condyle small subchondral fracture of the far posterior weightbearing aspect (likely site of impact).
  • Low grade edema is present along the central intercondylar region of the tibia but there is no significant tibia or fibula contusion.
  • At posterior horn-body junction of medial meniscus, there is edema with partial meniscocapsular separation and small ramp lesion (discussed in more detail in a future case)
  • Large knee joint effusion.

Overall, there is complete tear of PCL and MCL (reasons for showing this case). Other findings include suspected low grade ACL tear, significant osseous edema of the posterolateral femoral condyle with small subchondral impaction fracture of the posterior weightbearing lateral femoral condyle.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
PCL Tears:

PCL tears are much less common than ACL tears, and are most commonly seen in sports injuries and car accidents.

  • Although our example here demonstrates frank PCL rupture / discontiguity of fibres, most commonly the PCL fibres remain contiguous. You may see an enlarged, thickened PCL (> 7 mm in AP dimension -- see this AJR article), partial tearing, or secondary findings like posterior tibial translation.
  • Since PCL signal is usually uniformly low signal, high signal on PD is also common with tears. Be cognizant of meniscofemoral ligaments (i.e. Humphrey, Wrisberg) and do not mistaken those clefts for PCL tears
  • Indirect signs include bone contusions in anterior tibia and posterior femoral condyles (mechanism involves forced posterior displacement of tibia or anterior displacement of femur).


MCL Tears:

MCL injuries are common, usually due to valgus stress (i.e. opening of the medial knee). They are most commonly in the proximal or distal MCL. Most are non-operative, unless complete. They are classically graded as follows:

  • Grade 1 (minor sprain) - high signal superficial to ligament, which is otherwise intact / normal itself
  • Grade 2 (severe sprain OR partial tear) - high signal medial to ligament, partial disruption or high signal within ligament itself
  • Grade 3 - complete disruption of the ligament (like this case, which can be treated surgically)

In addition to grading, look for the following:

  • Associated injuries (ACL, PCL, meniscal tears, posteromedial corner injuries)
  • Reverse Segond fracture - an avulsion fracture from deep meniscotibial fibres with avulsion from medial proximal tibia; recall that Segond fractures in ACL tears involve lateral tibial plateau at lateral capsular ligament

cases.clinicalHistory: Plant and pivot in soccer, r/o MCL/ACL/medial meniscus
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 12 - Moderate grade MCL Tear (and ACL, meniscus)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Mild thickening and irregularity of the MCL with high signal superficial to it (see more superiorly and prox attacment) is in keeping with moderate grade partial tear

Other Findings:

  • High signal and discontinuity of most of the ACL fibres. A few posterolateral fibres remain intact.
  • Small knee joint effusion
  • An oblique longitudinal predominantly inferiorly-surfacing tear extends from the posterior horn of the medial meniscus (also see sag) into the body. No flipped fragment or bucket-handle.

In Summary, there is high-grade partial tearing of the ACL that may be functionally complete, oblique inferiorly-surfacing tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus, and moderate grade injury of MCL (reason for showing this case). This is referred to as the "terrible triad".

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced MCL injuries in a previous case. Recall the grading of MCL injuries:

  • Grade 1 (mild) - high signal superficial to ligament, with ligament intact
  • Grade 2 (moderate, partial) - high signal superficial to ligament with increased signal involving ligament itself or partial tearing / disruption (this case)
  • Grade 3 (severe, complete) - Complete disruption of the ligament

cases.clinicalHistory: Slip and fall, acute swelling, lachmann's positive? Lateral pain

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Reason for showing this case:

  • Thickening and heterogeneous signal along the fibular collateral ligament at its femoral insertion (also see Coronal) in keeping with moderate grade tear (grade 2 injury).
  • Conjoined and popliteus tendons are intact.

Other important findings:

  • ACL is markedly thickened, heterogeneous signal and is flattened with some of the of the fibres also flipped anteriorly in keeping with high-grade likely functional complete tearing along the proximal to mid fibres. There is subacute bone marrow contusions along the lateral tibial plateau and lateral femoral condyle (prior pivot-shift)
  • Edematous changes along the course of the MCL with no definite tearing suggestive of grade 1 injury. 
  • There is blunting of the apex of the body of the lateral meniscus in keeping with radial tear with suspected tiny horizontal component.
  • Focal partial meniscocapsular separation between the posterior horn-body junction of medial meniscus and the capsule
  • Moderate joint effusion with synovitis and no loose bodies.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
LCL tears are rare in isolation, usually associated with other ligament injuries (ACL, PCL, other posterolateral corner injuries).


LCL (Fibular Collateral Ligament) Injuries are often graded as follows:

  • Grade 1 - Fluid signal surrounding, no disruption
  • Grade 2 - partial discontinuity or signal within
  • Grade 3 - Complete disruption of the fibres

Always look for injury to other posterolateral corner structures, discussed in a future case in more detail.


Meniscocapsular Injuries:

These are frequently missed by radiologists, and can be a blind-spot for surgeons at arthroscopy. But, they can lead to an unstable meniscus and so are clinically important. Meniscocapsular injuries occur between the meniscus and its capsular attachments. Know the following:

  • More common medially
  • Medial: Between posterior horn of meniscus and capsule (like in this case), or an undersurface tear of medial meniscus in this region (ramp lesion)
  • Lateral: Wrisberg rip between posterior horn of lateral meniscus and ligament of Wrisberg, or separation between posterior horn and popliteomeniscal fascicle (shown previously)

Although beyond the scope here, this radiographics article covers these and other complex meniscal tears in more detail.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Posterolateral Corner:

  • There is a high-grade partial tear of the fibular collateral ligament adjacent to the femoral attachment (scroll down to here)
  • Additionally, there is an intermediate partial-thickness tear of the distal biceps tendon.
  • There is edema in the region of the arcuate ligament and popliteal fibular ligament suggesting an associated posterolateral capsular injury. Popliteus tendon is intact.
  • There is a moderate amount of edema along the posterolateral corner of the knee which surrounds the common peroneal nerve

Other findings:

  • Area of bone marrow edema along the medial aspect of the medial femoral condyle suggesting focal impaction injury with resultant bone bruise.
  • There is a intermediate grade partial thickness ACL injury.
  • Small lateral meniscal tears (subtle blunting)
  • Small joint effusion.

Posterolateral corner injury, with high-grade partial thickness tear of the fibular collateral ligament, intermediate grade partial thickness injury of the the distal biceps tendon, injury of the arcuate ligament/popliteal fibular ligament.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
The posterolateral corner is complex, with multiple named structures. However, there are only a 4 structures that you should focus on / should be aware of at this point.

  • LCL (lateral / fibular collateral ligament) - covered in video
  • Biceps Femoris Tendon
  • Popliteus Tendon
  • Popliteofibular / Arcuate Ligament Complex -- these are important structures but are hard to see in normal patients. Popliteofibular connects popliteus tendon to fibular head. Arcuate ligament is thickening of the capsule at the posterolateral corner, challenging to see in normal cases. Use the location of edema superficial to popliteal tendon here as a marker for injury

PLC injuries are common with ACL tears and are important to note, as an untreated injury can lead to instability and ACL graft failure.

cases.clinicalHistory: r/o patellar tendonitis, synovitis, inflammatory disease
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 15 - Jumper's Knee (Acute on chronic patellar tendinosis/tearing)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Marked thickening of the proximal patellar tendon with acute tear on a background of tendinosis. There is also partial tearing seen.
  • Partial tear involves approximately 30% of the medial-lateral and anterior-posterior dimension of the dorsum of the proximal patellar tendon immediately adjacent to the inferior pole of the patella
  • Surrounding marked edema/inflammation is seen within the infrapatellar fat pad, related to this acute tear with underlying tendinosis of the proximal patellar tendon.

Other findings:

  • Focal chondral fissure seen along the central aspect medial patellar articular facet in keeping with focal moderate chondromalacia patella in this location.
  • A sliver of fluid seen within the neck of a small popliteal/Baker's cyst.

Overall, there are findings of acute proximal patellar tearing on a background of tendinosis ("Jumpers knee"). Associated edema extending into the adjacent infrapatellar fat pad.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Jumper's Knee is a clinical syndrome that involves degeneration and/or tearing of patellar tendon at its patellar insertion. Other terms include patellar tendinosis or tendinopathy. Tendonitis is avoided because we don't often have histologic proof of true 'inflammation'.

It is common in jumping athletes (basketball, volleyball) but can also be seen in arthropathies.

On pathology, there are microtears and chronic inflammation/regeneration of the tendon.

MRI Findings are thickening of the proximal 1/3 of the tendon (increased AP diameter > 7 mm) and partial tearing of proximal patellar tendon, as we see in this case. Edema in Hoffa's (infrapatellar) fat pad can also be seen.


Treatment is usually conservative.

As an aside, this is often considered the adult version of Sinding-Larsen-Johansson.


Basic Terminology:

  • Tendinopathy - pathology in a tendon without specifying etiology (general term)
  • Tendinosis - suggests degenerative microtearing, usually chronic
  • Tearing - disruption of tendon fibres
  • Tendonitis - inflammation of the tendon, generally avoided on MRI unless histologic proof of true inflammation

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Quadriceps tendon complete rupture of the rectus femoris and vastus medialis and lateralis fibers, with maintenance of the vastus intermedius throughout most of its course.
  • However, a thin zone of focal full-thickness defect is identified through the distal vastus intermedius fibers.
  • Moderate joint effusion
  • Circumferential soft tissue edema

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Quadriceps Tendon Rupture is more common than patellar tendon rupture, usually due to forced flexion of the knee. Patients on steroids, with connective tissue disorders, diabetes, are at increased risk. Rupture usually occurs just above the patella.

Like in our case, rectus femoris, vastus medialis (VM), and vastus lateralis (VL) are often completely torn in quadriceps tendon tears. Vastus intermedius is more often partially torn than completely torn (see this paper). Tendon gaps of about 2-3 cm are often seen due to retraction (unopposed quad muscle action pull proximal stump of tendon up).

Recall the trilaminar anatomy of the quadriceps tendon: rectus femoris most superifcial, then VM/VL, then vastus intermedius deepest.

Treatment is often surgical.

cases.clinicalHistory: Prior ACL reconstruction, query ACL graft tear?

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Prior ACL graft reconstruction. ACL graft fibres remain intact with fibres visualized traversing the articulation from femoral to tibial tunnels.
  • Mild posterior deviation/bowing noted of the distal fibres of the ACL (also see here) suggestive of mild graft impingement. 
  • Essentially, there is mild bowing of the ACL fibres posteriorly, away from the intercondylar shelf of distal femur
  • Irregular low/intermediate signal intensity surrounding the ACL graft fibres consistent with mild arthrofibrosis in the intercondylar aspect of the articulation.

Other Findings:

  • Healed osseous fracture lateral margin proximal tibial metaphysis at site of prior Segond fracture.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
ACL Repair is common, with a goal of preventing joint instability. MRI is ideal for evaluating symptomatic ACL reconstructions. We briefly introduce normal and abnormal ACL reconstruction appearances below. This radiographics article covers in more detail.

Normal ACL Reconstruction:

  • Two most common grafts: bone-patellar-bone or hamstring graft.
  • Patellar tendon considered strongest, but more often get anterior knee pain
  • Four-strand hamstring graft (semitendinosus, gracilis) considered as strong although less favored in high-level athletes
  • Femoral and Tibial Tunnel locations are important.
  • Femoral tunnel should be as posterior as possible without disrupting posterior cortex of femur. The opening of the tunnel should be at 10 or 2 o'clock in the intercondylar notch in the coronal.
  • Tibial tunnel should be parallel to slope of intercondylar roof (Blumensaat Line), and opening just posterior to that line (like in our case) and at intercondylar eminence on Coronals
  • Post-operative (ranging up to 6 months to 4 years), high signal in the ACL graft may be due to ligamentization of the ACL graft. It is important to not overcall increased signal alone as pathology / tear.

Common Complications:

ACL Impingement:

  • Functional impairment of ACL graft due to interference with other structures, most commonly intercondylar roof
  • Roof Impingement is due to abnormal position of tibial tunnel (may be anterior to the intersection of Blumensaat Line and tibia)
  • Graft is in contact with anteroinferior margin of intercondylar roof and may be posteriorly bowed (like in our case) with increased signal in the graft

Partial Tear

Complete Tear - usually due to recurrent trauma

Arthrofibrosis - scar tissue in at least one compartment of the knee, can lead to reduced range of motion.

  • "Cyclops" lesion is a more localized nodular fibrous lesion due to arthrofibrosis; seen in up to 10% of patients post ACL repair (discussed in a future case)

Infection

Others


Types of Impingement

Based on location / structure interfering with ACL:

  • Roof Impingement is by far most common (described above), occurs at intercondylar roof. For practical purposes, this is the one you should know for now.
  • Intercondylar Sidewall impingement (usually tibial tunnel is too lateral)
  • PCL impingement (usually tibial tunnel is too medial)

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 18 - Prominent arthrofibrosis with large cyclops lesion

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• ACL graft fibres intact running in continuity from the femoral to tibial tunnels.
  • Prominent arthrofibrotic change surrounding the ACL graft in the intercondylar aspect of the articulation with a large nodular component extending anteriorly into the anterior aspect of the joint consistent with a large cyclops lesion.

Other Findings:

  • Prior hamstring autograft ACL reconstruction. Post harvest changes noted along the distal pes anserine tendons. There is also a lateral extra-articular tenodesis (LET) -- which is sometimes done alongside ACL repairs when there is lateral rotational instability -- here, they used part of the IT band.
  • Diminutive appearance posterior horn and body of the medial meniscus, consistent with prior partial meniscectomy.
  • Radial tearing at the junction posterior horn medial meniscus and posterior medial meniscal root insertion. Mild associated medial extrusion of the body of the medial meniscus from the medial joint line.
  • Marked thickening proximal femoral origin of the fibular collateral ligament consistent with prior partial injury.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A Cyclops Lesion is localized anterior athrofibrosis -- a complication of prior ACL reconstruction (about 5% of cases). It presents with anterior knee pain with extension +/- loss of extension. It represents a rounded fibrous mass in the anterior intercondylar notch, composed of granulation and fibrous tissue (signal will depend on how fibrous it is -- i.e. more fibrous will be lower T2 signal).

This is more common when ACL is repaired within 4 weeks of the injury (i.e. still inflamed).

Treatment can involve debridement.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Contusional edema in the medial aspect of the patella and lateral femoral condyle
  • There is a large osteochondral defect at the patellar apex measuring 9 mm in axial diameter and 10 mm craniocaudally. Notice the cartilage defect in addition to loss of normal cortical bone signal.
  • Small joint effusion
  • Loose osteochondral body overlies the medial femoral condyle.
  • There is also a focal full-thickness defect along the lateral aspect of the trochlea measuring 5 mm

Other notes:

  • TTTG distance is 13 mm (discussed in learning material).
  • There is mild trochlear dysplasia.

Summary:

Recent transient lateral patellar dislocation and relocation with classic contusional marrow edema. There is a large osteochondral defect at the patellar apex with 11 mm loose chondral fragment overlying the medial femoral condyle.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Patellar Dislocation usually occurs with knee twisting. You should know that it is almost always lateral, the classic contusion pattern (shown in this case), predisposing factors, and to look for associated cartilage defects of medial patella.

Normally, the patella is stabilized by passive (bones, ligaments), and active (muscles) stabilizers. Importantly, the femoral sulcus must be deep enough and the lateral trochlea high enough to prevent lateral dislocation. Medial ligaments (MPFL, medial retinaculum) also help prevent dislocation laterally.


Pattern of imaging findings post patellar dislocation are typical:

  • Contusion Pattern: Edema or deformity of inferomedial patella and lateral femoral condyle (like in our case -- picture the patella dislocating laterally and the kissing contusions that result when relocating)
  • MPFL disruption (70-100%), patellar lateralization

Other findings to look for:

  • Patellar chondral or osteochondral lesions of medial patella (seen in 2/3) -- most often concave impaction of inferomedial patella
  • Other cartilage defects (can occur elsewhere on the patella)
  • Lateral condylar edema / impaction

Predisposing factors:

  • Trochlear Dysplasia - shallow, flat, or convex trochlear groove +/- small lateral femoral condyle. Various measurements exist that you should read about. This excellent article goes into depth if interested. Measurements include:
  • Lateral Inclination Angle - measuring the inclination of the lateral trochlear facet (< 11 degrees considered abnormal / too flat) -- i.e. too flat [angle measured between these two lines]
  • Trochlear Facet Asymmetry - medial to lateral facet length
  • Trochlear Depth - deepest point of sulcus (abnormal if < 3 mm depth) -- i.e. 'shallow groove'
  • Patella Alta - high riding patella
  • Patellar Height Ratio: Patellar Tendon Length divided by Longest craniocaudal Patellar length (also called Insall-Salvati Index). Patella Alta if > 1.3 (normal is 1.1) -- i.e. the tendon is longer. We more commonly use the modified insall-salvati ratio, or the Caton-Deschamps index
  • TTTG (Tibial Tubercle to Trochlear Groove Distance) - the position of the tibial tubercle, if too lateral, can predispose to lateral dislocation (related to force vectors on patella from adjacent structures).
  • TTTG is measured as the medial-lateral distance between the centre of the tibial tuberosity and deepest part of trochlear groove. Since these are on different slices, the images need to be superimposed. >20mm is almost always associated with patellar instability, and 15-20 mm is borderline.


Management:

Geared towards ensuring reduction of the patella (though most self-reduce), fixing any large cartilage defects when possible, and correcting predisposing factors. Thus, it is important to look carefully for cartilage defects and predisposing factors.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Marrow edema in the anterior half of the medial condyle and the medial tibial plateau (also see coronal)
  • Flattening at the anterior margin of the medial femoral condyle consistent with a subchondral impaction injury with subchondral fracture -- at site of impact.
  • Further low-grade edema is seen at the posterolateral aspect of the lateral tibial plateau and lateral femoral condyle (see Sag of femoral condyle and tibial plateau)
  • Complete rupture of the distal fibular collateral ligament (follow from superior to inferior). There is an intrasubstance splitting the distal biceps femoris tendon, which is detached from the fibula.

Other findings:

  • Subcutaneous edema is seen predominately over the lateral aspect of the knee, with fluid tracking through the posterior compartment.
  • ACL is of increased signal and partially distorted, predominantly the mid and inferior fibers, consistent with a partial-thickness injury.
  • Inferior surfacing longitudinal oblique tear involving the posterior horn and body of the medial meniscus.

Overall:

  • Pattern of bone marrow edema is consistent with valgus impaction with a subchondral impaction fracture at the anterior margin of the medial femoral condyle.
  • Full-thickness injury of fibular collateral ligament, biceps femoris and popliteofibular ligament

Other findings as described.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case demonstrates a predominant pattern of impaction medially (medial tibial plateau and femoral condyle) with fracture -- and lateral opening of the joint. Some refer to this as a varus force, but understanding the mechanism is more important. In addition to reviewing the findings here and pattern of injury, we also discuss common "bone contusion patterns" in the knee below.

Common Bone Contusion Patterns in the Knee:

  1. Pivot-Shift - covered previously. Valgus stress with knee in flexion results in anterior translation of tibia, ACL tear, and impaction of midportion of lateral femoral condyle onto posterolateral tibial plateau (see prior case demonstrating this pattern of kissing contusions)
  2. Dashboard Injury - force to anterior tibia when knee is flexed (like when dashboard of car hits knee in accident). Edema is seen in anterior aspect of tibia, associated with PCL rupture.
  3. Hyperextension Injury - force to anterior tibia when knee is extended (kicked, car bumper vs pedestrian leg). At hyperextension, anterior tibial plateau hits anterior femoral condyle (and associated kissing contusions seen). If valgus force + hyperextension, then the kissing contusions will be mostly medial.
  4. Clip Injury - pure valgus stress when knee is in flexion -- so marrow edema is mostly lateral (lateral femoral condyle), +/- minor changes medially due to MCL avulsion (medial knee opens up, often with MCL injury).
  5. Lateral Patellar Dislocation - transient lateral dislocation of the patella resulting in anterolateral femoral condyle and inferomedial patellar kissing contusions (demonstrated in prior case).
  6. Valgus and Varus force Injuries -- refers to the site of impact (medial in varus force and lateral in varus). Our case is a varus force (from medial).

The most classic questions relate to Pivot-Shift and Lateral Patellar Dislocation (1, 5), but understanding 2-4 can help elucidate pattern of injury in cases such as this.

cases.clinicalHistory: 3 weeks post lateral impaction injury with persistent restricted extension

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Prominent bone bruising is seen within the anterior aspects of the lateral femoral condyle and lateral tibial plateau
  • Edema involving far anterior margin of the medial femoral condyle and medial tibial plateau, to a lesser extent

Pattern of bone marrow edema is suggestive of hyperextension injury.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced the most common patterns of bone contusion at the knee in the previous case, including hyperextension injury. To recap:

  • Direct force to anterior tibia with foot planted can result in hyperextension
  • Pattern of kissing contusions in anterior tibial plateau and anterior femoral condyle -- often greater on the lateral side (but if valgus stress also present, then can be more medial)

See this image.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Osteo-chondral lesion is seen involving the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle (also see axial, sag PD, Sag T2) This lesion measures approximately 1.5 cm in medial lateral extent and 2.6 cm in anterior-posterior dimension.
  • Hyaline articular cartilage overlying this lesion is intact, with signal irregularity of the subchondral bone and mild subchondral edema seen surrounding the lesion -- but not as high signal as joint fluid.
  • The patient is skeletally immature with incompletely fused physes
  • No osteochondral lesion on the right

Findings in a skeletally immature patient are in keeping with a stable OCD lesion on the left. We cover OCD and criteria for instability below.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Terminology can be confusing with osteochondral abnormalities and related conditions. You must keep these straight:

  • "Osteochondral Lesion" is a general term that refers to abnormality of subchondral bone/marrow and articular cartilage (hence osteo[bone] - chondral [cartilage]).
  • "Osteochondral Defect" refers to a more localized defect of cartilage and subchondral bone.
  • There are many conditions that result in an 'osteochondral defect'. This includes the distinct idiopathic entity, "OCD" (Osteochondritis Dissecans), demonstrated in this case and discussed below. We also introduce other entities that can lead to osteochondral defects (acute trauma, subchondral insufficiency fracture/SONK, AVN, osteoarthritis).


OCD (Osteochondtitis Dissecans):

A distinct idiopathic entity that occurs in younger patients (childhood to middle age). You should know the following:

  • "Inside-Out" mechanism - starts in subchondral bone, and then subsequently can involve articular cartilage (unlike trauma, which involves cartilage first and then bone -- i.e. outside-in)
  • Classic location - lateral (intercondylar) aspect of the medial femoral condyle (like in our case)
  • Stability of the lesion is key for prognosis, and important to note.

Classic Findings of Instability:

  • High signal at interface between lesion and remaining bone on T2
  • Cystic change deep to lesion
  • High signal line extending through articular cartilage overlying the lesion
  • Defect filled with joint fluid (indicating prior complete detachment of the fragment)

However, in skeletally immature patients (such as this patient), there are additional stipulations:

  • High signal at interface between lesion and bone only indicates instability IF:
  • T2 signal same as joint fluid,
  • Second outer rim of T2W low signal
  • Multiple breaks in subchondral bone on T2
  • Cysts only indicate instability if > 5 mm

In our case, there is high T2 signal surrounding the fragment, but not as high as joint fluid -- thus NOT indicating instability. Articular cartilage overlying is intact (hence technically stable).

teachingSections.name: Intro: Other Causes of Osteochondral Lesions
teachingSections.html:
Acute Traumatic

  • 'Bone bruise' and 'contusion' refer to trabecular microfractures that look like bone marrow edema (no discrete fracture line)
  • Subchondral fracture will appear as a subchondral linear hypointensity (on T1), without involvement of articular surface
  • Osteochondral Fracture - involves both articular cartilage and subchondral bone (fracture line involves both), with subchondral bone plate depression / extension into the joint.

Subchondral Insufficiency Fracture (SIF) [Previously SONK / spontaneous osteonecrosis of knee]

We discuss this in greater detail in a future case, but introduce the main ideas here (repetition is key):

  • Occurs in older patients with sudden onset knee pain without trauma
  • Due to a physiologic force on weak bone (often osteopenic) --> subchondral fracture, which can either stabilize or collapse
  • Usually occurs on weight-bearing central aspect of femoral condyle (but can involve tibial plateau)
  • Appears as marked bone marrow edema, hypointense line in subarticular marrow parallel to bone plate
  • May see collapse (fracture) or fluid cleft underlying (when unstable/separation)
  • "SONK" is a misnomer -- previously thought to be primarily due to osteonecrosis. However, in reality, SIF can lead to secondary necrosis post collapse

AVN (Primary Osteonecrosis):

  • May be due to trauma, steroid use, sickle cell, or other
  • Osteonecrosis is a general term that should be used. "AVN" of the knee involves epiphysis / subchondral bone near the articular surface. Anywhere else in the bone was previously referred to as "bone infarct"
  • We cover the appearance in future cases

OCD

  • Discussed in this case

Osteochondral Lesions in Arthritis

  • Conditions involving cartilage can involve subchondral bone as well, including in OA with bone marrow edema-like lesions

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Right Knee:

  • Osteochondral lesion involving the inner weightbearing aspect of the medial femoral condyle (also see Sag PD)
  • There is a fluid cleft undermining the lesion, with multiple cysts at the interface
  • There is full-thickness chondral fissuring at the margins of the osteochondral fragment
  • Depression of the osteochondral fragment

Findings indicate an Unstable OCD Lesion with underlying edema and cystic change, depression of fragment.

Other Findings:

  • Peripheral undersurface fraying of the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus.
  • RIGHT: Multi focal full-thickness chondral fissuring particularly involving the medial patellar facet and central femoral trochlea.
  • LEFT: There is mild patellofemoral chondrosis with focal areas of full-thickness cartilage loss in the medial and lateral facets.
  • Small baker's cyst

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced Osteochondral Lesions, Osteochondral Defects, and conditions that lead to osteochondral defects in the previous case. This is another case of the distinct entity, OCD (Osteochondritis Dissecans), but with features of instability.

Recall for OCD:

  • "Inside-Out" mechanism involving subchondral bone and then progressing to involve cartilage
  • Classic location on intercondylar aspect of the medial femoral condyle
  • Features of instability depend on skeletal maturity (in this case, skeletally mature).

Classic Findings of Instability:

  • High signal at interface between lesion and remaining bone on T2
  • Cystic change deep to lesion
  • High signal line extending through articular cartilage overlying the lesion (fissuring seen in this case)
  • Defect filled with joint fluid (indicating prior complete detachment of the fragment) -- in our case, there is depression of the fragment

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Severe chondrosis involving the weightbearing surface of the medial femoral condyle and medial tibial plateau (articular cartilage is gone, bone on bone here)
  • Areas of subchondral bone marrow edema but no evidence of an insufficiency fracture.
  • There is focal severe chondrosis involving the posterior aspect of the weightbearing surface of the lateral femoral condyle with underlying bone changes (compare to articular cartilage here). There is also focal chondrosis involving the weightbearing surface of the lateral tibial plateau with subchondral changes

Other Findings:

  • Radial tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus extending into posterior root.
  • There is trace blunting of the free edge of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus. This extends into the midbody. The mid body is extruded. The anterior horn remains intact.
  • There is degenerative fraying of the free edge of the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus. The posterior horn and is decreased in size when compared with the anterior horn.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Osteoarthritis (OA) of the knee is common, primarily involving degeneration of articular cartilage -- usually asymmetric. Classic radiographic (Xray) findings include asymmetric joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, osteophyte formation, subchondral cysts. But, MRI can directly show the morphology of articular cartilage. Though knee MRI is NOT indicated for moderate to severe OA seen on Xray, you will commonly see features of OA on MRI -- and so it is important to recognize them and describe cartilage findings accurately.

Characteristic Features of OA on MRI:

  • Articular Cartilage Loss - including fissuring (linear defects perpendicular to cartilage surface), moderate loss of cartilage, and full thickness loss of cartilage.
  • Subarticular Bone Marrow Lesions - mostly high signal / edema-like (though sclerosis can also be seen on MRI), subchondral cystic change. Subarticular lesions indicate full thickness cartilage loss.
  • Osteophytes - outgrowths of bone at articular margins, usually the first sign of OA.
  • Joint Effusion. May see synovitis (due to secondary inflammation), which can be symptomatic.
  • Meniscal Tears, including with extrusion of meniscus, are associated -- with tears seen in 50-90%. With severe OA, there are almost always meniscal tears, often complex, multidirectional, and degenerative. These are usually non-surgical.

Description of Cartilage Defects in Reports:

  • "Chondromalacia" refers to softening of the cartilage seen on arthroscopy; generally avoided when describing cartilage on MRI.
  • "Grade 1 - 4" isn't clear on its own, should be avoided in reports.
  • **Remain descriptive -- i.e. fissuring, cartilage loss; and include severity
  • Severity:
  • Superficial Partial Thickness (<50% thickness)
  • Deep partial thickness (>50% thickness)
  • Full thickness (full thickness, with marrow changes).
  • Descriptive Terms:
  • Fissure (crack/linear)
  • Loss (chronic decrease in thickness)
  • Defect (localized loss of height)
  • Fibrillation (superficial surface irregularity)
  • Chondrosis is a more general term referring to cartilage pathology.
  • Practical Approach to terminology:
  • Chondrosis often used for thinning of cartilage
  • Isolated fissuring
  • Delamination (horizontal fissuring along the margin) which can lead to a chondral flap
  • Defect for focal lesions

cases.clinicalHistory: SLE and persistent bilateral knee pain and swelling

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Right knee:

  • Extremely large joint effusion. Notice the prominent superior band/plica (synovial fold)
  • Moderate synovitis -- notice how adjusting the windowing can increase conspicuity of the frond-like hypertrophy (compare with default windowing). Also see elsewhere (compare with default).
  • Other: There is focal full thickness chondrosis of the posterior aspect the weightbearing surface of the lateral femoral condyle.

Left knee:

  • There is an extremely large joint effusion. There is an incomplete superior plica band.
  • There is moderate to severe synovitis
  • Other: Multifocal chondrosis, not the reason for showing this case.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Synovitis refers to inflammation of the synovium -- which can occur at joints, bursae, or along tendon sheaths. Etiologies include infectious, non-infectious inflammatory (inflammatory arthritis), degenerative (OA), foreign body, and others.

On MRI, synovial thickening is the hallmark, often seen as irregular, frond-like hypertrophy like in this case. With gadolinium, there is avid enhancement of synovium.

In this case, there are no prominent erosions. Severe synovitis without erosions is common in Lupus (SLE). Look up Jaccoud's arthropathy -- referring to reversible deformity (on exam) and synovitis with lack of erosions, seen in Lupus. In RA, you would expect erosions with this much inflammation.

Always consider infection first -- they will aspirate and exclude infection before giving steroids. Infection often has extensive peri-articular inflammation / edema in surrounding tissues.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 26 - Subchondral insufficiency fracture (SIF), Previously SONK

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Significant marrow edema in the medial femoral condyle, with subchondral linear hypointensity on the weightbearing aspect (also seen on non FS)
  • No articular collapse

Findings are consistent with a subchondral insufficiency fracture (SIF) involving weightbearing aspect of medial femoral condyle (condition previously known as 'SONK').

Other Findings:

  • An oblique inferior surfacing tear is seen involving the mid body of the medial meniscus. This extends to the posterior horn. No displaced meniscal fragments.
  • Mild thickening and intermediate signal intensity involving the femoral origin of the anterior cruciate ligament, compatible with a prior partial thickness injury.
  • Moderate chondrosis involving the medial tibiofemoral articular cartilage. Mild lateral tibiofemoral chondrosis is also demonstrated. Moderate chondrosis is seen involving the medial articular facet of the patella, and the central femoral trochlea.
  • Small joint effusion and reactive soft tissue edema

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This is a case of Subchondral Insufficiency Fracture (SIF), previously SONK (spontaneous osteonecrosis of knee).

SIF occurs in older patients, who often present with sudden onset knee pain without significant trauma. SIF occurs due to a physiologic force on weak bone (often osteopenic). This leads to subchondral fracture, which can either stabilize or collapse.

Know the following:

  • Usually occurs on weight-bearing central aspect of femoral condyle, like in this case, but can involve tibial plateau.
  • Appears as marked bone marrow edema, hypointense line in subarticular marrow parallel to bone plate (like in our case)
  • May see collapse (fracture) or fluid cleft underlying (when unstable/separation) -- not seen in this case.

"SONK" is a misnomer -- previously thought to be primarily due to osteonecrosis (hence Spontaneous Osteonecrosis of the Knee). However, in reality, SIF can lead to secondary necrosis post collapse -- and so this is not a 'primary osteonecrosis'.

cases.clinicalHistory: Bilateral Knee Pain, hx of scleroderma. AVN?

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Right Knee:

  • Osteonecrosis involving distal femoral metaphysis and proximal tibial metaphysis
  • Each has central marrow signal (fatty), and a peripheral serpentine demarcation
  • Double line sign peripherally demonstrates continuous T2W hyperintensity and areas of hypointense sclerosis surrounding.
  • Osteonecrosis extends to the intercondylar aspect of the proximal tibia in the region of the tibial spines, however no subchondral fracture or collapse seen in this region
  • No focal marrow edema seen surrounding the areas of avascular necrosis to suggest acute change and the well-defined serpiginous margins of the areas of osteonecrosis suggest a more chronic nature of the changes seen.

Left Knee:

  • Similar areas of chronic mature avascular necrosis tibial and femoral diametaphyses, as described above.
  • Focal area avascular necrosis inferior lateral aspect of the patella with surrounding marrow edema which appears more subacute in nature. No associated subchondral collapse or overlying articular cartilage abnormality seen.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Osteonecrosis is a general, encompassing term referring to ischemia/infarction of bone.

  • "AVN" (Avascular Necrosis) was traditionally used for epiphyseal/subarticular involvement.
  • "Bone Infarct" was traditionally used metaphyseal involvement.

Regardless, cut off of blood supply to part of bone results in infarction -- with a central necrotic core surrounded by granulation tissue between necrotic and viable bone. This gives rise to the typical appearance:

  • Central signal usually fatty / marrow signal, surrounded by surrounding serpiginous interface line
  • Double Line Sign - serpentine line around the periphery of necrotic bone has an inner continuous T2W bright signal (granulation tissue) and outer T2W dark (sclerotic) appearance. This is pathognomonic. Also see on PD.
  • Acute infarcts have more ill defined T2W hyperintensity, with chronic more geographic rim with rim of sclerosis
  • Peripheral rim may enhance post-gad


Osteonecrosis can be seen in a variety of conditions, including but not limited to:

  • Trauma (i.e. fracture that disrupts blood supply, like femoral neck fracture)
  • Hemoglobinopathies (i.e. Sickle Cell)
  • Connective Tissue Disorders (SLE, systemic sclerosis/scleroderma, etc)
  • Corticosteroids, Chemotherapy, Radiation
  • Pregnancy related (femoral head)
  • Gaucher's
  • Alcohol

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Moderate to large joint effusion with complex heterogeneous areas of low signal. These changes are predominantly in the suprapatellar recess, intercondylar notch, and extra-articular extension through the posterior capsule with extension in the popliteal fossa adjacent to the neurovascular bundle
  • Blooming artifact on GRE sequence (compare with T2W same site)
  • Similar changes at the origins of the lateral and medial heads of the gastrocnaemius tendons

Other Findings:

  • The small areas of erosive changes in the lateral patellar facet head increased compared to prior exam. The erosive changes in the posterior aspect of the lateral femoral condyle and lateral tibial plateau are not significantly changed.
  • Horizontal, oblique inferior-surfacing tear in the posterior horn of the medial meniscus with a meniscal defect superiorly. Mild apical blunting of the mid body of the lateral unchanged.
  • A small area of moderate to severe chondrosis in the lateral tibial plateau. Small area of moderate chondrosis in the intercondylar aspect of the lateral femoral condyle. Small areas of severe chondrosis in the medial femoral trochlea and lateral patellar facet.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
PVNS (Pigmented Villonodular Synovitis) is considered a benign neoplastic process involving synovium. It involves progressive proliferation and hypertrophy of synovium, which becomes nodular.

It is characterized by hemosiderin deposition -- which is responsible for its imaging appearance. Although most people still say PVNS when involving a joint, it is now called Diffuse Tenosynovial Giant Cell Tumor.


It is most commonly mono-articular (involves one joint), most often the Knee but can involve other joints, bursae, tendon sheath (i.e. "GCT of tendon sheath").

MRI Findings are Diagnostic:

  • Synovial irregular thickening -- with a lobulated, frond-like or mass-like appearance
  • Areas of low signal due to hemosiderin deposition (though often heterogeneous signal -- higher signal indicates less hemosiderin)
  • GRE (gradient echo) sequences are sensitive to magnetic susceptibility artifact -- and so you see blooming artifact due to hemosiderin deposition (appears larger and darker on GRE). This is classic / pathognomonic for PVNS - see note in Ddx below.
  • Effusion
  • EXTRINSIC erosion of bone on both sides of the joint, often with sclerotic margins, but joint itself spared
  • Marked enhancement of synovium (if contrast given)


Differential Considerations:

  • Synovial hemangioma and hemophiliac arthropathy may also show blooming artifact due to repetitive intraarticular hemorrhage and synovial hemosiderin deposition.
  • However, diffuse intraarticular PVNS can be distinguished from these conditions because it is not associated with either serpentine vascular channels (hemangioma) or a clinical history of hemophilia.


For residents -- PVNS is a very common exam question!

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Lobulated well-circumscribed ovoid lesion within the superomedial recess of the knee along the juxta-articular aspect of the medial femoral condyle measuring up to 3.9 cm.
  • The lesion is heterogeneously hypointense on PD images, and heterogeneously T2 hyperintense, with small lobular hypointense foci centrally within it.
  • Demonstrates relatively homogeneous enhancement (compare with pre-contrast)
  • The lesion causes some slight smooth remodelling along the medial femoral condyle, with a tiny area of apparent erosion at the anterior aspect of the lesion (and T1 fat sat series 7 image 15). 

Other findings:

  • Moderate sized joint effusion with synovitis and avid enhancement of the synovium.
  • Study was not optimized for assessment of the internal structures of the knee.

SUMMARY:

3.9 cm lobulated well-circumscribed avidly enhancing low signal intensity intra-articular lesion within the superomedial knee recess adjacent to the medial femoral condyle. This was focal nodular synovitis.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Focal Nodular Synovitis is localized/focal PVNS (previous case was 'diffuse PVNS'. It presents with a single synovial nodule, most often in the knee. The histologic process is the same as described for the last case of PVNS, with cellular proliferation and hemosiderin deposition (though often less hemosiderin deposition).

The most up to date term is "Localized Tenosynovial Giant Cell Tumor".


Important things to know:

  • Most frequently involves knee, more often anterior compartment (like our case)
  • Unlike diffuse PVNS, localized form less heterogeneous and more often more T2 hyperintense (like our case)
  • Hemosiderin deposition can be minimal in localized PVNS

Definitive diagnosis made by tissue sampling.

Surgical resection is primary treatment.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Large joint effusion, predominately suprapatellar
  • Numerous small T2 hypointense foci demonstrated within this effusion in keeping with rice bodies
  • **Notice how much more conspicuous these are vs default windowing. This reinforces the importance of appropriate windowing when evaluating joint fluid and synovium.
  • Moderate diffuse synovial thickening
  • No meniscal tear

SUMMARY:

Moderate synovial thickening with large joint effusion containing multiple small rice bodies. Findings are common in inflammatory arthropathy.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Rice Bodies are small fragments of hypertrophic synovium -- that undergo necrosis and slough off into the joint. They are often seen with chronic synovial inflammation. They look like grains of rice (hence the name), and either fill the joint or layer dependently. Synovium often enhances, but the rice bodies themselves will not.


They are most often seen with chronic inflammation: rheumatoid arthritis, JIA (juvenile idiopathic arthritis), TB, other inflammatory arthropathies and infections.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Large joint effusion
  • Frond-like masses composed of fat (see non-FS and FS images; also here and here). These masses follow the signal of fat on all sequences. These are centred suprapatellar / patellofemoral.

Other findings:

  • Bone marrow signal is unremarkable. No evidence of bony erosion.
  • No synovitis
  • Other chronic changes, not the reason for showing this case

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Lipoma Arborescens is rare, involving the synovial lining of joints (or bursae). It is characterized by frond-like deposition of fat (lipoma) and thought to resemble a tree (hence arborescens, which means tree-like in latin).

The most common location is the knee, most often suprapatellar (like this case), and usually unilateral.

They are sporadic, but can be associated with findings of OA or collagen vascular disorders.

Note: "Frond" means leaf-like.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Mass within the infrapatellar fat pad (Hoffa's fat pad)
  • MRI Signal: Heterogeneous signal intensity on both T1 & T2 weighted sequences with areas of well-defined areas of low signal with ring/arc/curvilinear morphology, related to ossification/calcification
  • Enhancement: Heterogeneous enhancement
  • Size: measures up to 4.7 cm

Relationships:

  • Lesion abuts the anterior cortex proximal tibial metaphysis without intraosseous invasion.
  • Exerts mass effect on the distal patellar tendon which is bowed anteriorly.
  • Focal anterior lateral extension through the fibres of the lateral retinaculum

Other findings:

  • Small associated right knee joint effusion and mild prepatellar bursitis. Mild enhancement of synovium
  • Chondrosis with chondral flap tearing patellar apex and lateral patellar articular facet

SUMMARY:

Heterogeneous mass centred within the infrapatellar (Hoffa's) fat pad. This was previously biopsied and is consistent with para-articular chondroma. Mass effect noted upon the distal patellar tendon with focal transfascial extension lateral retinaculum

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Para-articular chondroma (also intracapsular chondroma) is very rare. The point of showing this case is to illustrate the appearance of a soft tissue tumor and to teach general principles, rather than to focus on this particular entity.

General Principles: Tumors

  • Aggressive on imaging ≠ malignant. Tumors can be aggressive or non-aggressive based on imaging appearances. This does not mean malignant versus benign. Aggressive patterns usually reflect rapid growth but not synonymous with malignant. Some malignant tumors can look deceptively indolent.
  • Always look at radiographs/CT. They are best for matrix/mineralization (i.e. ring and arc calcs), ossification; and should be interpreted in conjunction with MRI.
  • Soft Tissue Masses: "Definitive" vs "Indeterminate". On MRI, a meaningful subset of lesions can be called definitively (e.g. simple cyst, lipoma, peripheral nerve sheath tumor, focal PVNS, vascular malformation). Masses without specific benign features should be treated as indeterminate and worked up at a sarcoma centre, often with biopsy.
  • Why give contrast? Post-contrast MRI helps differentiate solid from cystic lesions; and can separate solid/viable tissue from cystic or necrotic components. This can guide the biopsy target (see below)
  • Biopsy targeting principle. Aim for solid, enhancing/viable tumor and avoid non-enhancing necrotic or hemorrhagic zones to reduce nondiagnostic sampling. (PET/CT, when available, can help target metabolically active regions)

Aside: Aggressive bone-lesion features:

  • Ill-defined/wide zone of transition, cortical destruction, aggressive periosteal reaction (lamellated/spiculated/Codman), associated soft-tissue mass


Apply these to our case:

In our case, we have a soft tissue lesion that has clear solid enhancing components, necrotic areas, and is not a lesion we would call 'definitive' (cyst, lipoma, etc). So, it requires biopsy -- which was performed and came back as intracapsular chondroma. This entity is discussed below.


Specific Diagnosis:

Intracapsular chondroma is a rare extraskeletal chondroma that occurs due to chondroid metaplasia of the joint capsule. It most often involves the knee, often inferior to patella.

Imaging appearance:

  • Well circumscribed lobulated mass near the joint capsule (where it arises), +/- smooth erosion of adjacent bone
  • T2W hyperintense with low signal areas of calcification/ossification (ring and arc pattern typical of chondroid matrix; curvilinear or stippled)
  • Peripheral pattern of enhancement
